---
title: "Roadmap"
subtitle: "Your journey to a first break in AI"
description: "Step-by-step learning path: set up a Quarto blog, run models locally, learn inference and training, build an AI product, and ship a capstone project."
css: styles/roadmap.css
---

Your learning path — one journey, step by step. Cohort 01 ran **1 May 2026 — 30 June 2026** and is now **archived**. Steps 1 and 2 are fully documented. Steps 3 onwards continue in **[FBA Lab](https://bubblnet.com/)** — hands-on, no GPU or setup required. Use your AI-based IDE and the community to complete each step.

::: {.callout-important}
## Before you begin
Make sure you've completed the [Checklist](checklist.qmd) — set up your accounts so you can ask questions and share progress as you go.
:::

::: {.view-toggle}
<button class="active" onclick="toggleView('visual')">Visual</button>
<button onclick="toggleView('normal')">Normal</button>
:::

<!-- ════════════════  VISUAL VIEW  ════════════════ -->

:::: {.roadmap-visual .active}

::: {.timeline}

::: {.timeline-step data-step="1"}
::: {.step-card}
[Step 1]{.step-label}

### First use of AI for coding

Set up a **Quarto blog** and host it on GitHub with an about-me page, blog posts, "Today I learned," and other pages.

<details>
<summary><strong>Show details ↓</strong></summary>

<p><strong>Tasks:</strong></p>
<ol>
<li>Set up the project locally, link it to a GitHub repo, and configure GitHub Pages for deployment.</li>
<li>Use your AI-based IDE to complete this setup.</li>
</ol>

<p><strong>You will learn:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li>GitHub basics refresher — branches, commits, PRs, merge conflicts</li>
<li>Setting up a personal and blogging website with Quarto</li>
<li>How AI coding tools and SWE agents work in practice</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Lessons:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><a href="lessons/lesson-0-welcome.qmd">Welcome to First Break AI</a> — cohort intro video with interactive transcript (start here).</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Office Hours sessions:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-3-fba-cli">The firstbreakai CLI — validate your Quarto blog from inside the repo</a> (5 Jun, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-2-fba-agentic-widget">Agentic First Break AI — the cohort widget and the roadmap-as-LMS</a> (5 Jun, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-5-markdown-knowledge-base">Markdown everywhere — and Karpathy's AI-managed knowledge base</a> (8 May, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-13.qmd#topic-1-github-collaboration">GitHub collaboration — PRs, conflicts, rebasing</a> (13 Mar)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-2-claude-code-harness-leak">Claude Code harness — skills, sub-agents, safe learning</a> (10 Apr)</li>
</ul>

</details>
:::
:::

::: {.timeline-step data-step="2"}
::: {.step-card}
[Step 2]{.step-label}

### Run a model locally

Run Qwen3 0.6B locally in pure C — trace every operation from tokenization to token output.

<details>
<summary><strong>Show details ↓</strong></summary>

<p><strong>You will learn:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li>Basics of inference: decoding, KV cache, sampling</li>
<li>Chat templates and system prompts (ChatML format, special tokens)</li>
<li>Tokenization — why subword units, vocabulary size trade-offs</li>
<li>Temperature, top-p, and how they control output randomness</li>
<li>GGUF vs SafeTensors — model file formats and quantization</li>
<li>Transformer architecture: self-attention, MHA, positional encoding, decoder-only design</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Lessons:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><a href="lessons/lesson-1-huggingface-beyond-upload.qmd">Lesson 1 — HuggingFace Beyond Upload</a>: why an open model is not a file — it's a supply chain (provenance, datasets, security, evals, runtime, agents).</li>
<li><a href="lessons/lesson-1b-qwen3-fundamentals.qmd">Lesson 1b — Qwen3 Fundamentals</a>: how a language model lives on disk and loads into memory — GGUF file format, memory mapping, tensor layout, SafeTensors vs GGUF, and the full conversion pipeline.</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Guides:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><a href="blog/qwen3-run-locally.qmd">Run Qwen3 0.6B in pure C</a> — inference from first principles (tokenization, chat templates, attention, KV cache).</li>
<li><a href="blog/model-formats-gguf-safetensors.qmd">GGUF vs SafeTensors</a> — model weight formats, security, quantization, and why we start with pure C.</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Learning resources</strong> — follow in this order:</p>
<ol>
<li><a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=80bIUggRJf4&amp;t=38s" target="_blank"><strong>KV Cache explained</strong> (video, ~10 min)</a> — start here. Visual walkthrough of why the KV cache exists, how it grows with context length, and what practical models do to keep it small. Directly answers why GPU memory is the bottleneck in inference.</li>
<li><a href="https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-transformer/" target="_blank"><strong>The Illustrated Transformer</strong> — Jay Alammar</a> — the most referenced visual guide to transformer architecture. Covers embeddings, self-attention, multi-head attention, and positional encoding with animated diagrams. Read this before the paper — it will make the paper feel simple.</li>
<li><a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762" target="_blank"><strong>Attention Is All You Need</strong> (2017 paper)</a> — the original transformer paper. After reading the Alammar blog, this is approachable. The key contributions: removal of recurrence, self-attention as a core operator, multi-head attention, positional encoding. Shorter than you expect (~15 pages).</li>
<li><a href="https://x.com/JustinLin610/status/2037116325210829168" target="_blank"><strong>Agentic models are the future</strong> — Junyang Lin</a> — former Qwen team lead on why reasoning/thinking models are a stepping stone, and why agentic models (RL-trained, tool-using, multi-step) are where the field is heading. Read this last — it gives you a mental map of where everything you are learning is going.</li>
</ol>

<p><strong>Office Hours sessions:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-9-sampling-deep-dive">Sampling deep dive — temperature, top-p, and the "original" 1.4% surprise</a> (5 Jun, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-8-token-viz">Live token-probability visualisation — top-20 over a 151,936-token vocab</a> (5 Jun, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-10-chat-templates">Chat templates and special tokens — talking to the model</a> (5 Jun, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-11-setup-walkthrough">Setup walkthrough — recursive clone, model download, make run, WSL</a> (5 Jun, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-1-agentic-blender">Agentic AI in action — Claude + Blender + FBA MCP build a 3D GGUF tower</a> (5 Jun, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-1-three-intuitions">Three intuitions — model shape, autoregressive generation, vocab-wide probabilities</a> (8 May, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-2-why-qwen3-0-6b">Why Qwen3 0.6B — small but structurally identical to large models</a> (8 May, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-3-huggingface">HuggingFace — the GitHub for models (LFS → Xet, model cards)</a> (8 May, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-4-config-and-transformers">config.json and the Transformers library</a> (8 May, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-7-pure-c-demo">Live demo — Qwen3 in pure C with GGUF, system prompt, thinking, multi-turn</a> (8 May, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-4-what-to-expect">What to expect — Qwen3 locally, HF basics, tokenizer, embeddings, temperature, thinking on/off</a> (8 May, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-13.qmd#topic-2-qwen3-inference-concepts">Qwen3 inference concepts — temperature, chat templates, speculative decoding</a> (13 Mar)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-1-why-attention-lstm-and-rnn-limitations">Why attention? LSTM and RNN limitations</a> (27 Mar)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-2-transformer-architecture-breakthroughs">Transformer breakthroughs — self-attention, MHA, positional encoding</a> (27 Mar)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-3-decoder-only-architecture-in-modern-llms">Decoder-only architecture in modern LLMs</a> (27 Mar)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-4-model-taxonomy">Model taxonomy — dense vs MoE, precision, thinking vs agentic</a> (27 Mar)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-3-kv-cache-caveats">KV cache — caveats after the explainer</a> (10 Apr)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-4-benchmark-literacy">Benchmark literacy — reading model cards</a> (10 Apr)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-10-rope-and-training-script">RoPE in practice — RoFormer paper and code</a> (10 Apr)</li>
</ul>

</details>
:::
:::

::: {.timeline-step data-step="3"}
::: {.step-card}
[Step 3]{.step-label}

### Inference deep dive

Go beyond running a model — understand how inference works under the hood and how to serve models at scale.

*This step continues in [FBA Lab](https://bubblnet.com/) — hands-on, no setup required.*

<details>
<summary><strong>Show details ↓</strong></summary>

<p><strong>You will learn:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li>Inference engines and runtimes (vLLM, TGI, llama.cpp server)</li>
<li>Batching, continuous batching, and throughput vs. latency trade-offs</li>
<li>Quantization (GGUF, GPTQ, AWQ) and when to use each format</li>
<li>Speculative decoding — how draft models speed up large model inference</li>
<li>Structured output, function calling, and tool use</li>
<li>Serving and API design for inference endpoints</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Lessons:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><a href="lessons/lesson-2a-run-coding-ai-locally.qmd">Lesson 2a — Run a Coding AI on Your Laptop</a>: serve Qwen2.5 Coder with llama.cpp server and wire Cline to a local OpenAI-compatible endpoint.</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Office Hours sessions:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-13.qmd#topic-4-unsloth-and-llm-efficiency">Unsloth and LLM efficiency</a> (13 Mar)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-5-benchmarking-in-ai">Benchmarking in AI</a> (27 Mar)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-6-the-three-pillars-of-model-development">The three pillars of model development</a> (27 Mar)</li>
</ul>

</details>
:::
:::

::: {.timeline-step data-step="4"}
::: {.step-card}
[Step 4]{.step-label}

### Training fundamentals

Build the foundations to train and fine-tune models from scratch — from a single GPU to distributed multi-node setups.

*This step continues in [FBA Lab](https://bubblnet.com/) — hands-on, no setup required.*

<details>
<summary><strong>Show details ↓</strong></summary>

<p><strong>You will learn:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li>PyTorch fundamentals: tensors, autograd, training loops</li>
<li>Modelling: architectures (transformers, attention, MLP), building blocks from scratch</li>
<li>Data pipelines: datasets, dataloaders, preprocessing, tokenization at scale</li>
<li>Fine-tuning: LoRA, QLoRA, full fine-tune, adapters — when to use each</li>
<li>Distributed training: DDP, FSDP, multi-GPU and multi-node setups</li>
<li>Experiment tracking and evaluation (Weights &amp; Biases, validation loss curves)</li>
<li>Parallelism strategies: tensor parallelism, pipeline parallelism, expert parallelism</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Guides:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><a href="blog/reading-the-curves-llm-training.qmd">Reading the Curves (LLM training)</a> — W&amp;B checklists, loss spikes, OLMo checkpoints, Marin 32B + QK-Norm warm-start.</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Projects you'll be ready for:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li>nanoGPT speedrun — train GPT-2 scale to a target validation loss as fast as possible</li>
<li>Megatron / Picotron — read and understand production-grade distributed training code</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Office Hours sessions:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-2-training-trajectory">From a random model to Qwen3 0.6B — the training trajectory</a> (8 May, Cohort 01)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-7-data-parallelism-and-the-parallelism-ladder">DDP and the parallelism ladder</a> (27 Mar)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-8-project-watch-speedrun-and-auto-research-gpt">Speedrun and Auto Research GPT</a> (27 Mar)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-6-speedrun-nanogpt">Speedrun — nanoGPT, target loss, Tyler Romero's worklog</a> (10 Apr)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-7-ddp">DDP — gradients and all-reduce</a> (10 Apr)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-8-communication-and-deepseek">Communication bubble, NVLink, DeepSeek-style constraints</a> (10 Apr)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-9-muon-optimizer">Muon optimizer — from contest to production</a> (10 Apr)</li>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-10-rope-and-training-script">Training script walkthrough — ranks, loops, collectives</a> (10 Apr)</li>
</ul>

</details>
:::
:::

::: {.timeline-step .coming-soon data-step="5"}
::: {.step-card}
[Step 5 — coming soon]{.step-label}

### Build an AI product

Ship an AI-powered product end to end — from idea to deployed, monitored application.

*This step continues in [FBA Lab](https://bubblnet.com/) — hands-on, no setup required.*

<details>
<summary><strong>Show details ↓</strong></summary>

<p><strong>You will learn:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li>Product thinking: problem → solution → users — scoping a project that can be shipped</li>
<li>Building with APIs, RAG, agents, and tool use</li>
<li>Frontend/backend integration for AI features</li>
<li>Deployment, monitoring, and iteration — keeping it running after launch</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Office Hours sessions:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><a href="office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-1-learner-project-lan-stock-game">Learner project — LAN stock game (scope and shipping)</a> (10 Apr)</li>
</ul>

</details>
:::
:::

::: {.timeline-step .coming-soon data-step="6"}
::: {.step-card}
[Step 6 — coming soon]{.step-label}

### Capstone project or open-source contribution

Prove what you've learned. Pick one: ship a capstone project or make a meaningful contribution to an open-source AI project.

*This step continues in [FBA Lab](https://bubblnet.com/) — hands-on, no setup required.*

<details>
<summary><strong>Show details ↓</strong></summary>

<p><strong>Options:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Capstone:</strong> End-to-end project combining inference, training, or product skills — deployed, documented, and added to your public portfolio</li>
<li><strong>Open-source contribution:</strong> Submit a PR to an AI repo (model, library, dataset, docs) — get reviewed, merged, and credited</li>
<li>Present your work to the cohort; get peer feedback</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Why it matters:</strong> A shipped project or merged PR is the strongest signal on your profile when applying for your first AI role.</p>

</details>
:::
:::

:::

::::

<!-- ════════════════  NORMAL VIEW  ════════════════ -->

:::: {.roadmap-normal}

::: {.step-normal}
### Step 1: First use of AI for coding — Quarto blog with GitHub

**Goal:** Create a Quarto blog and host it on GitHub with an about-me page, blog posts, "Today I learned," and other pages.

1. Set up the project locally, link it to a GitHub repository, and configure GitHub Pages for deployment.
2. Use your AI-based IDE to complete this setup.

**Learning objectives:** GitHub basics refresher · Setting up a personal and blogging website · Understanding how coding tools or SWE / AI agents work

**Lessons:**

- [Welcome to First Break AI](lessons/lesson-0-welcome.qmd) — cohort intro video with interactive transcript (start here).

**Office Hours sessions:**

- [The firstbreakai CLI — validate your Quarto blog from inside the repo](office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-3-fba-cli) (5 Jun, Cohort 01)
- [Agentic First Break AI — the cohort widget and the roadmap-as-LMS](office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-2-fba-agentic-widget) (5 Jun, Cohort 01)
- [Markdown everywhere — and Karpathy's AI-managed knowledge base](office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-5-markdown-knowledge-base) (8 May, Cohort 01)
- [GitHub collaboration — PRs, conflicts, rebasing](office-hours/2026-03-13.qmd#topic-1-github-collaboration) (13 Mar)
- [Claude Code harness — skills, sub-agents, safe learning](office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-2-claude-code-harness-leak) (10 Apr)
:::

::: {.step-normal}
### Step 2: Run a model locally — Basic inference setup

**Goal:** Run Qwen3 0.6B locally in pure C — trace every operation from tokenization to token output.

**Topics:** Basics of inference (decoding, KV cache) · Chat templates and system prompts · Tokenization · Temperature and sampling · GGUF vs SafeTensors · Transformer architecture (self-attention, MHA, positional encoding, decoder-only design)

**Lessons:**

- [Lesson 1 — HuggingFace Beyond Upload](lessons/lesson-1-huggingface-beyond-upload.qmd) — why an open model is not a file. The supply-chain mental model: provenance, datasets, security (`trust_remote_code`), evals, runtime routing, and agent-readable manifests.
- [Lesson 1b — Qwen3 Fundamentals](lessons/lesson-1b-qwen3-fundamentals.qmd) — how a language model lives on disk and loads into memory. GGUF file format, memory mapping, tensor layout, SafeTensors vs GGUF, and the full conversion pipeline.

**Guides:**

- [Run Qwen3 0.6B in pure C](blog/qwen3-run-locally.qmd) — inference from first principles.
- [GGUF vs SafeTensors](blog/model-formats-gguf-safetensors.qmd) — model weight formats, security, quantization, and why we start with pure C.

**Learning resources** — follow in this order:

1. [**KV Cache explained** (video, ~10 min)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=80bIUggRJf4&t=38s) — why the KV cache exists, how it grows with context length, and what practical models do to keep it small.
2. [**The Illustrated Transformer** — Jay Alammar](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-transformer/) — the most referenced visual guide to transformer architecture. Read before the paper.
3. [**Attention Is All You Need** (2017 paper)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762) — the original transformer paper. Approachable after the Alammar blog.
4. [**Agentic models are the future** — Junyang Lin](https://x.com/JustinLin610/status/2037116325210829168) — former Qwen team lead on why agentic models (not just thinking models) are where the field is heading.

**Office Hours sessions:**

- [Sampling deep dive — temperature, top-p, and the "original" 1.4% surprise](office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-9-sampling-deep-dive) (5 Jun, Cohort 01)
- [Live token-probability visualisation — top-20 over a 151,936-token vocab](office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-8-token-viz) (5 Jun, Cohort 01)
- [Chat templates and special tokens — talking to the model](office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-10-chat-templates) (5 Jun, Cohort 01)
- [Setup walkthrough — recursive clone, model download, make run, WSL](office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-11-setup-walkthrough) (5 Jun, Cohort 01)
- [Agentic AI in action — Claude + Blender + FBA MCP build a 3D GGUF tower](office-hours/2026-06-05.qmd#topic-1-agentic-blender) (5 Jun, Cohort 01)
- [Three intuitions — model shape, autoregressive generation, vocab-wide probabilities](office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-1-three-intuitions) (8 May, Cohort 01)
- [Why Qwen3 0.6B — small but structurally identical to large models](office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-2-why-qwen3-0-6b) (8 May, Cohort 01)
- [HuggingFace — the GitHub for models (LFS → Xet, model cards)](office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-3-huggingface) (8 May, Cohort 01)
- [config.json and the Transformers library](office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-4-config-and-transformers) (8 May, Cohort 01)
- [Live demo — Qwen3 in pure C with GGUF, system prompt, thinking, multi-turn](office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-7-pure-c-demo) (8 May, Cohort 01)
- [What to expect — Qwen3 locally, HF basics, tokenizer, embeddings, temperature, thinking on/off](office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-4-what-to-expect) (8 May, Cohort 01)
- [Qwen3 inference concepts — temperature, chat templates, speculative decoding](office-hours/2026-03-13.qmd#topic-2-qwen3-inference-concepts) (13 Mar)
- [Why attention? LSTM and RNN limitations](office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-1-why-attention-lstm-and-rnn-limitations) (27 Mar)
- [Transformer breakthroughs — self-attention, MHA, positional encoding](office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-2-transformer-architecture-breakthroughs) (27 Mar)
- [Decoder-only architecture in modern LLMs](office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-3-decoder-only-architecture-in-modern-llms) (27 Mar)
- [Model taxonomy — dense vs MoE, precision, thinking vs agentic](office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-4-model-taxonomy) (27 Mar)
- [KV cache — caveats after the explainer](office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-3-kv-cache-caveats) (10 Apr)
- [Benchmark literacy — reading model cards](office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-4-benchmark-literacy) (10 Apr)
- [RoPE in practice — RoFormer paper and code](office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-10-rope-and-training-script) (10 Apr)
:::

::: {.step-normal}
### Step 3: Inference deep dive

*This step continues in [FBA Lab](https://bubblnet.com/) — hands-on, no setup required.*

**Goal:** Go beyond running a model — understand how inference works under the hood and how to serve models.

**Topics:** Inference engines and runtimes (vLLM, TGI, llama.cpp server) · Batching and continuous batching · Quantization (GGUF, GPTQ, AWQ) · Structured output, function calling, and tool use · Serving and API design for inference endpoints

**Lessons:**

- [Lesson 2a — Run a Coding AI on Your Laptop](lessons/lesson-2a-run-coding-ai-locally.qmd) — serve Qwen2.5 Coder with llama.cpp server and wire Cline to a local OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

**Office Hours sessions:**

- [Unsloth and LLM efficiency](office-hours/2026-03-13.qmd#topic-4-unsloth-and-llm-efficiency) (13 Mar)
- [Benchmarking in AI](office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-5-benchmarking-in-ai) (27 Mar)
- [The three pillars of model development](office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-6-the-three-pillars-of-model-development) (27 Mar)
:::

::: {.step-normal}
### Step 4: Training fundamentals

*This step continues in [FBA Lab](https://bubblnet.com/) — hands-on, no setup required.*

**Goal:** Build the foundations to train and fine-tune models from scratch.

**Topics:** PyTorch fundamentals (tensors, autograd, training loops) · Modelling (transformers, attention, MLP) · Data pipelines · Fine-tuning (LoRA, QLoRA, full fine-tune, adapters) · Distributed training (DDP, FSDP, multi-GPU) · Experiment tracking and evaluation

**Guides:**

- [Reading the Curves (LLM training)](blog/reading-the-curves-llm-training.qmd) — W&B checklists, loss spikes, OLMo checkpoints, Marin 32B and QK-Norm warm-start.

**Office Hours sessions:**

- [From a random model to Qwen3 0.6B — the training trajectory](office-hours/2026-05-08.qmd#topic-2-training-trajectory) (8 May, Cohort 01)
- [DDP and the parallelism ladder](office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-7-data-parallelism-and-the-parallelism-ladder) (27 Mar)
- [Speedrun and Auto Research GPT](office-hours/2026-03-27.qmd#topic-8-project-watch-speedrun-and-auto-research-gpt) (27 Mar)
- [Speedrun — nanoGPT, target loss, Tyler Romero's worklog](office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-6-speedrun-nanogpt) (10 Apr)
- [DDP — gradients and all-reduce](office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-7-ddp) (10 Apr)
- [Communication bubble, NVLink, DeepSeek-style constraints](office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-8-communication-and-deepseek) (10 Apr)
- [Muon optimizer — from contest to production](office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-9-muon-optimizer) (10 Apr)
- [Training script walkthrough — ranks, loops, collectives](office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-10-rope-and-training-script) (10 Apr)
:::

::: {.step-normal}
### Step 5: Build an AI product (coming soon)

*This step continues in [FBA Lab](https://bubblnet.com/) — hands-on, no setup required.*

**Goal:** Ship an AI-powered product end to end.

**Topics:** Product thinking · Building with APIs, RAG, agents, and tool use · Frontend/backend integration · Deployment, monitoring, and iteration

**Office Hours sessions:**

- [Learner project — LAN stock game (scope and shipping)](office-hours/2026-04-10.qmd#topic-1-learner-project-lan-stock-game) (10 Apr)
:::

::: {.step-normal}
### Step 6: Capstone project or open-source contribution (coming soon)

*This step continues in [FBA Lab](https://bubblnet.com/) — hands-on, no setup required.*

**Goal:** Prove what you've learned. Pick one: build a capstone project or make a meaningful contribution to an open-source AI project.

**Options:** Capstone (end-to-end project, deployed and documented, added to public portfolio) · Open-source contribution (PR to an AI repo — model, library, dataset, or docs) · Present your work to the cohort for peer feedback
:::

::::

More steps can be added as the roadmap grows. Suggest new modules via [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/thefirehacker/firstbreakai/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) or a pull request.

```{=html}
<script>
function toggleView(mode) {
  document.querySelector('.roadmap-visual').classList.toggle('active', mode === 'visual');
  document.querySelector('.roadmap-normal').classList.toggle('active', mode === 'normal');
  document.querySelectorAll('.view-toggle button').forEach(function(btn) {
    btn.classList.toggle('active', btn.textContent.trim().toLowerCase() === mode);
  });
}

// Scroll-triggered fade-in for timeline steps
(function() {
  var steps = document.querySelectorAll('.timeline-step');
  if (!steps.length) return;
  var observer = new IntersectionObserver(function(entries) {
    entries.forEach(function(e) {
      if (e.isIntersecting) {
        e.target.classList.add('visible');
        observer.unobserve(e.target);
      }
    });
  }, { threshold: 0.15 });
  steps.forEach(function(s) { observer.observe(s); });
})();

// ── Progress overlay ──
(function() {
  var MCP_URL = 'https://fba-mcp.throbbing-thunder-4d33.workers.dev';
  var LESSON_MAP = {
    'lesson-0-welcome': '0',
    'lesson-1-huggingface-beyond-upload': '1',
    'lesson-1b-qwen3-fundamentals': '1b',
    'lesson-2a-run-coding-ai-locally': '2a'
  };

  function getToken() {
    return localStorage.getItem('fba_token');
  }

  function injectBanner(type, html) {
    var container = document.querySelector('.view-toggle');
    if (!container) return;
    var banner = document.createElement('div');
    banner.className = type;
    banner.innerHTML = html;
    container.parentNode.insertBefore(banner, container);
    return banner;
  }

  function markStepCompleted(stepNum) {
    // Visual view
    var vizSteps = document.querySelectorAll('.timeline-step[data-step="' + stepNum + '"]');
    vizSteps.forEach(function(el) {
      el.setAttribute('data-completed', 'true');
      var badge = document.createElement('span');
      badge.className = 'step-completed-badge';
      badge.textContent = '\u2713';
      el.appendChild(badge);
    });

    // Normal view
    var normalSteps = document.querySelectorAll('.step-normal');
    normalSteps.forEach(function(el) {
      var h3 = el.querySelector('h3');
      if (!h3) return;
      var match = h3.textContent.match(/Step\s+(\d+)/);
      if (match && match[1] === String(stepNum)) {
        el.setAttribute('data-completed', 'true');
      }
    });
  }

  function addValidateHint(stepNum) {
    // Visual view
    var vizSteps = document.querySelectorAll('.timeline-step[data-step="' + stepNum + '"]');
    vizSteps.forEach(function(el) {
      var card = el.querySelector('.step-card');
      if (!card || card.querySelector('.step-validate-hint')) return;
      var hint = document.createElement('div');
      hint.className = 'step-validate-hint';
      hint.innerHTML = 'Validate: <code>firstbreakai validate ' + stepNum + '</code>';
      card.appendChild(hint);
    });

    // Normal view
    var normalSteps = document.querySelectorAll('.step-normal');
    normalSteps.forEach(function(el) {
      var h3 = el.querySelector('h3');
      if (!h3) return;
      var match = h3.textContent.match(/Step\s+(\d+)/);
      if (match && match[1] === String(stepNum)) {
        if (el.querySelector('.step-validate-hint')) return;
        var hint = document.createElement('div');
        hint.className = 'step-validate-hint';
        hint.innerHTML = 'Validate: <code>firstbreakai validate ' + stepNum + '</code>';
        el.appendChild(hint);
      }
    });
  }

  function addLessonCheckboxes(completedLessons) {
    var links = document.querySelectorAll('a[href*="lessons/"]');
    links.forEach(function(a) {
      if (a.parentNode.querySelector('.lesson-checkbox')) return;
      var href = a.getAttribute('href') || '';
      var lessonFile = href.replace(/.*lessons\//, '').replace(/\.qmd$/, '').replace(/\.html$/, '');
      var lessonId = LESSON_MAP[lessonFile];
      if (!lessonId) return;

      var cb = document.createElement('input');
      cb.type = 'checkbox';
      cb.className = 'lesson-checkbox';
      cb.checked = completedLessons.indexOf(lessonId) !== -1;
      cb.setAttribute('data-lesson-id', lessonId);

      cb.addEventListener('change', function() {
        var token = getToken();
        if (!token) { cb.checked = !cb.checked; return; }
        fetch(MCP_URL + '/widget/mcp', {
          method: 'POST',
          headers: {
            'Content-Type': 'application/json',
            'Authorization': 'Bearer ' + token
          },
          body: JSON.stringify({
            jsonrpc: '2.0', id: 1,
            method: 'tools/call',
            params: { name: 'do', arguments: { intent: 'mark_lesson', lesson_id: lessonId } }
          })
        }).catch(function() { cb.checked = !cb.checked; });
      });

      a.parentNode.insertBefore(cb, a);
    });
  }

  function renderProgress(profile) {
    if (!profile) {
      return;
    }

    // Remove login banner if present
    var existing = document.querySelector('.roadmap-login-banner');
    if (existing) existing.remove();

    // Step completions
    var completedSteps = [];
    if (profile.step_completions && Array.isArray(profile.step_completions)) {
      profile.step_completions.forEach(function(sc) {
        if (sc.passed === 1 || sc.passed === true) {
          completedSteps.push(Number(sc.step));
          markStepCompleted(sc.step);
        }
      });
    }

    // Validate hints for steps 0-2 not completed
    [0, 1, 2].forEach(function(s) {
      if (completedSteps.indexOf(s) === -1) {
        addValidateHint(s);
      }
    });

    // Lesson checkboxes
    var completedLessons = profile.lesson_completions || [];
    addLessonCheckboxes(completedLessons);
  }

})();
</script>
```
